title: Thermodynamic processes
description: Let's solve some problems to better understand the change in thermodynamic quantities (such as work done, heat supplied and change in internal energy) during thermodynamic processes (such as isothermal expansion, adiabatic expansion, etc.)

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes isothermal expansion.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W $ [[☃ dropdown 1]]  $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

The internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on its temperature. The internal energy increases or decreases depending on whether the temperature increases or decreases respectively.

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. When the gas expands, is the area positive or negative?

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $Q$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\underbrace{\text{Isothermal}}_{T\ \text{constant}}\ \gray{\text{Expansion}}$.

Since the temperature $T$ is constant, the internal energy cannot change. Thus, $\Delta U =0$.

$\gray{\text{Isothermal}}\ \underbrace{\text{Expansion}}_{V\ \text{increases}}$.

Since the volume is increasing, the $PV$ curve moves towards the right.

[[☃ image 1]]

Thus the area which equals the work done by the gas $W$ is positive $\Rightarrow W>0$.

$\Delta U = 0$ and $W>0$

$\Rightarrow Q=\Delta U + W$ must also be greater than zero!

*Sense check: $Q>0$ implies heat is being added to the system. This energy could have increased the temperature and thus internal energy of the gas, but isothermal conditions are maintained by doing this process very, very slowly.  This means all of the input heat is used by the gas to do positive work!*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U =0$

- $W>0$

- $Q>0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes isothermal compression.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]  $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

The internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on its temperature. The internal energy increases or decreases depending on whether the temperature increases or decreases respectively.

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. When the gas contracts, is the area positive or negative?

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $Q$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\underbrace{\text{Isothermal}}_{T\ \text{constant}}\ \gray{\text{Compression}}$.

Since the temperature $T$ is constant, the internal energy cannot change. Thus, $\Delta U =0$.

$\gray{\text{Isothermal}}\ \underbrace{\text{Compression}}_{V\ \text{decreases}}$.

Since the volume is decreasing, the $PV$ curve moves towards the left.

[[☃ image 1]]

Thus the area which equals the work done by the gas $W$ is negative $\Rightarrow W<0$.

$\Delta U = 0$ and $W<0$

$\Rightarrow Q=\Delta U + W$ must also be less than zero!

*Sense check: $Q<0$ implies heat is being removed from the system. This energy could have decreased the temperature and thus internal energy of the gas, but isothermal conditions are maintained by doing this process very, very slowly. This means all of the heat being extracted is provided by the external agency doing work on the system!*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U =0$

- $W<0$

- $Q<0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes adiabatic compression.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed. What happens in an adiabatic process?

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. When the gas contracts, is the area positive or negative?

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $\Delta U$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\underbrace{\text{Adiabatic}}_{Q=0}\ \gray{\text{Compression}}$.

An adiabatic process is one where no heat is exchanged with the system $\Rightarrow Q=0$.

$\gray{\text{Adiabatic}}\ \underbrace{\text{Compression}}_{V\ \text{decreases}}$.

Since the volume is decreasing, the $PV$ curve moves towards the left.

[[☃ image 1]]

Thus the area which equals the work done by the gas $W$ is negative $\Rightarrow W<0$.

Using first law, $Q=\Delta U + W$ must equal zero. We know the work done is negative. So $\Delta U$ must be positive for the sum to be zero. $\Rightarrow \Delta U>0$ 

*Sense check: $\Delta U>0$ implies the internal energy and thus the temperature of the gas must be increasing. But since no heat is exchanged, where is this energy coming from? It comes from the external work being done on the system.*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U >0$

- $W<0$

- $Q=0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes adiabatic expansion.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$ 

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed. What happens in an adiabatic process?

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. When the gas expands, is the area positive or negative?

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $\Delta U$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\underbrace{\text{Adiabatic}}_{Q=0}\ \gray{\text{Expansion}}$.

An adiabatic process is one where no heat is exchanged with the system $\Rightarrow Q=0$.

$\gray{\text{Adiabatic}}\ \underbrace{\text{Expansion}}_{V\ \text{increases}}$.

Since the volume is increasing, the $PV$ curve moves towards the right.

[[☃ image 1]]

Thus the area which equals the work done by the gas $W$ is positive $\Rightarrow W>0$.

Using first law, $Q=\Delta U + W$ must equal zero. We know the work done is positive. So $\Delta U$ must be negative for the sum to be zero. $\Rightarrow \Delta U<0$ 

*Sense check: $\Delta U<0$ implies the internal energy and thus the temperature of the gas must be decreasing. But since no heat is exchanged, where is this energy going? It goes into doing external work.*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U <0$

- $W>0$

- $Q=0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes an isochoric process. During the process, the pressure exerted by the gas $P$ increases.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. What is the area under the curve for an isochoric process?

Using $PV=nRT$, we can determine how the temperature varies. Since temperature is proportional to the internal energy, the variation of temperature also tells us how $U$ changes.

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $Q$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\underbrace{\text{Isochoric}}_{V=\text{constant}}\ \gray{\text{Process}}$.

An isochoric process is one where volume is constant. So, the $PV$ diagram is a vertical line as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area under the curve is zero and thus, $W=0$.

Since the volume is constant and pressure is increasing, we can say using the ideal gas equation that the temperature of the gas must be increasing.

$\stackrel{\text{increasing}}{\overbrace{T}}=\dfrac{\stackrel{\text{increasing}}{\overbrace{P}}\quad\stackrel{\text{constant}}{\overbrace {V}}}{nR}$

This means the internal energy $U$ must also be increasing. Thus, $\Delta U >0$.

$\Delta U > 0$ and $W=0$

$\Rightarrow Q=\Delta U + W$ must also be greater than zero!

*Sense check: $Q>0$ implies heat is being added to the system. This energy could have done work by expanding the gas but isochoric conditions imply that the volume of the gas must be constant.  This means all of the input heat is used to increase the internal energy of the gas! Imagine a pressure cooker with no valve!*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U>0$

- $W=0$

- $Q>0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes an isochoric process. During the process, the pressure exerted by the gas $P$ decreases.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. What is the area under the curve for an isochoric process?

Using $PV=nRT$, we can determine how the temperature varies. Since temperature is proportional to the internal energy, the variation of temperature also tells us how $U$ changes.

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $Q$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\underbrace{\text{Isochoric}}_{V=\text{constant}}\ \gray{\text{Process}}$.

An isochoric process is one where volume is constant. So, the $PV$ diagram is a vertical line as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area under the curve is zero and thus, $W=0$.

Since the volume is constant and pressure is decreasing, we can say using the ideal gas equation that the temperature of the gas must be decreasing.

$\stackrel{\text{decreasing}}{\overbrace{T}}=\dfrac{\stackrel{\text{decreasing}}{\overbrace{P}}\quad\stackrel{\text{constant}}{\overbrace {V}}}{nR}$

This means the internal energy $U$ must also be decreasing. Thus, $\Delta U<0$.

$\Delta U < 0$ and $W=0$

$\Rightarrow Q=\Delta U + W$ must also be less than zero!

*Sense check: $Q<0$ implies heat is being extracted from the system. Since no work is done, this energy that leaves the system comes at the expense of internal energy of the gas! Imagine a closed container that is cooling down!*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U<0$

- $W=0$

- $Q<0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes expansion under isobaric conditions.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. When the gas expands, is the area positive or negative?

Using $PV=nRT$, we can determine how the temperature varies. Since temperature is proportional to the internal energy, the variation of temperature also tells us how $U$ changes.

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $Q$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\gray{\text{Isobaric}}\ \underbrace{\text{Expansion}}_{V\ \text{increases}}$.

Since the volume is increasing, the $PV$ curve moves towards the right.

[[☃ image 1]]

Thus the area which equals the work done by the gas $W$ is positive $\Rightarrow W>0$.

$\underbrace{\text{Isobaric}}_{P=\text{constant}}\ \ \underbrace{\text{Expansion}}_{V\ \text{increases}}$.


Since the volume is increasing (expansion) and pressure is constant, we can say using the ideal gas equation that the temperature of the gas must be increasing.

$\stackrel{\text{increasing}}{\overbrace{T}}=\dfrac{\stackrel{\text{constant}}{\overbrace{P}}\quad\stackrel{\text{increasing}}{\overbrace {V}}}{nR}$

This means the internal energy $U$ must also be increasing. Thus, $\Delta U>0$.

$\Delta U > 0$ and $W>0$

$\Rightarrow Q=\Delta U + W$ must also be greater than zero!

*Sense check: $Q>0$ implies heat is being added to the system. Part of this energy goes to increasing the temperature of the system and the rest of the energy does positive work.*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U >0$

- $W>0$

- $Q>0$

assessment_items.question_content:
A gas undergoes compression under isobaric conditions.

**Choose the signs of the thermodynamic quantities during this process?**  
*Note: $W$ represents work done by the system.*

$\Delta U$ [[☃ dropdown 4]] $0$

$W$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $0$

$Q$ [[☃ dropdown 3]] $0$
hints:
##Strategy

The work done by a gas $W$ is given by the area under the curve in a $PV$ diagram. When the gas contracts, is the area positive or negative?

Using $PV=nRT$, we can determine how the temperature varies. Since temperature is proportional to the internal energy, the variation of temperature also tells us how $U$ changes.

Using the above two along with the first law of thermodynamics, $Q=\Delta U + W$ will tell us whether $Q$ is positive or negative.

##Solution

$\gray{\text{Isobaric}}\ \underbrace{\text{Compression}}_{V\ \text{decreases}}$.

Since the volume is decreasing, the $PV$ curve moves towards the left.

[[☃ image 1]]

Thus the area which equals the work done by the gas $W$ is negative $\Rightarrow W<0$.

$\underbrace{\text{Isobaric}}_{P=\text{constant}}\ \ \underbrace{\text{Compression}}_{V\ \text{decreases}}$.


Since the volume is decreasing (compression) and pressure is constant, we can say using the ideal gas equation that the temperature of the gas must be decreasing.

$\stackrel{\text{decreasing}}{\overbrace{T}}=\dfrac{\stackrel{\text{constant}}{\overbrace{P}}\quad\stackrel{\text{decreasing}}{\overbrace {V}}}{nR}$

This means the internal energy $U$ must also be decreasing. Thus, $\Delta U<0$.

$\Delta U < 0$ and $W<0$

$\Rightarrow Q=\Delta U + W$ must also be less than zero!

*Sense check: $Q<0$ implies heat is being taken out of the system. Part of this energy comes from the internal energy of the system and the rest comes from the work done by the external agent on the system.*

The signs of the thermodynamic quantities are:

- $\Delta U<0$

- $W<0$

- $Q<0$
